title: Genome Reduction — Finding the Minimum by Subtraction

lines.text: Section four. Genome reduction — finding the minimum by subtraction.
Natural minimal cells set a lower bound on what life can be in the wild.
But the more aggressive question is: of the genes in even the smallest known cell, are some of them still unnecessary? Can we reduce further?
This is the minimal genome project, and it has been pursued since the 1990s under the leadership of Nobel Laureate Hamilton Smith, working largely at the J. Craig Venter Institute.
The project focuses on Mycoplasma genitalium and its 482 genes.
Even within those 482 genes, M. genitalium has already shed a great deal compared to a typical bacterium.
It has the essential central-dogma machinery — DNA replication, transcription, and translation.
It has core metabolism — phospholipid biosynthesis, vitamin utilisation, glycolysis.
But it has done away with a great deal as well.
It cannot make most of its own amino acids — it parasitises hosts for those.
It cannot make most of its own nucleic acid precursors.
It lacks the enzymes of oxidative metabolism.
It has remarkably few regulatory genes — almost no transcription factors, almost no two-component signalling systems.
M. genitalium is, in evolutionary terms, a cell that has already pruned itself heavily.
Of those 482 genes, which are absolutely required for the cell to survive in the lab?
Researchers approach this question in two complementary ways.
First, the comparative approach.
Compare the genomes of many organisms — close relatives and distant — and look for genes that are conserved across the largest possible variety of life.

Genes that show up in every cell, with very similar sequences, are likely essential — they are essential because evolution would have removed them by now if they were not, and the lack of sequence change indicates strong selective pressure to keep them.

Comparing all sequenced genomes, the surprising answer is only about 60 to 70 genes are shared across all of life — much lower than the actual minimal-cell gene count, simply because different organisms have evolved different gene families to perform the same essential functions.
DNA polymerase in a human cell is not closely related to DNA polymerase in a bacterium, even though both perform the same function.
Narrowing the comparison to close relatives — say, the Mycoplasma genus alone — gives a more realistic estimate of around 200 to 300 essential genes.
Second, the reductionist approach.
Instead of comparing sequences, you experimentally remove genes one at a time and see what happens.
In M. genitalium, this was done by transposon insertion — a transposon is a mobile DNA element that disrupts whatever gene it lands in.

Researchers inserted transposons into thousands of cells and then asked: which cells survived? Genes that always show transposon insertions in survivors are non-essential; genes that never tolerate insertions are essential.
In M. genitalium, this approach identified about 100 genes as non-essential, leaving an estimated 370 or so essential genes.
Strikingly, about 100 of those 370 essential genes have no known function — we know they are required for survival, but we do not know what they do.
That gap in understanding is one of the open puzzles of the minimal-cell project.
Similar experiments in Bacillus subtilis — a much larger bacterium with around 4,800 genes — identified 271 essential genes individually.
But the authors are careful to note: removing each gene one at a time and observing survival is not the same as removing all non-essential genes simultaneously.
A cell that tolerates losing gene A or gene B alone may not survive losing A and B together.
Synthetic-lethal interactions abound in biology, and the simple per-gene knockout data underestimate the dependencies.
The endgame of the minimal-genome project is a strain called M. laboratorium — a synthetic Mycoplasma with as few genes as possible, custom-built to serve as a clean chassis for synthetic biology.
And the route the researchers chose for building M. laboratorium is not sequential deletion from a natural genome.
It is bottom-up genome synthesis — chemically writing the entire minimal genome from scratch, which is the topic of the next section.
